content.text: What Is Control Flow

Control flow is the order in which individual statements and function calls execute in a program. By default, Python executes statements sequentially from top to bottom. Control flow structures let you divert from that default: conditionals choose between paths, loops repeat a block, break and continue alter loop execution, and exception handling transfers control when an error occurs. Python 3.10 added structural pattern matching (match/case) as a fourth major control-flow mechanism.

Every non-trivial program uses control flow. Without it, a program could only compute a single fixed sequence of steps — no branching, no repetition, no error recovery. With it, a single function can handle thousands of different input scenarios and gracefully recover from failures that would otherwise crash the program.

Python's control-flow syntax is deliberately minimal and readable. Blocks are delimited by indentation rather than braces, which means the visual structure of the code directly reflects the logical structure. This makes Python control flow easier to audit and less prone to the classic off-by-one brace errors found in C-family languages.
Why Control Flow Matters

In agentic AI systems, control flow is the backbone of agent execution loops. An agent repeatedly checks whether a goal is achieved (conditional), calls tools in sequence (loop), handles tool errors gracefully (try/except), and matches tool responses to known response types (match/case). Understanding Python's control-flow primitives is not optional — it is the literal mechanism by which an agent makes decisions.

Error handling in particular is critical for reliability. An agent that calls external APIs, file systems, or language models will encounter errors: network timeouts, rate limits, malformed responses, insufficient permissions. Wrapping these calls in try/except blocks with appropriate recovery logic is what separates a toy agent from a production-grade one.
Benefits of Python's Control Flow
Indentation-delimited blocks eliminate ambiguity: the visual structure is the logical structure.
Chained comparisons (1 < x < 10) avoid double evaluation and read like mathematical notation.
for-else and while-else clauses handle the 'did the loop complete without a break?' pattern cleanly.
try/except/else/finally gives fine-grained control over error handling and cleanup.
match/case (Python 3.10+) supports destructuring patterns that would require multiple isinstance checks otherwise.
Walrus operator (:=) lets you assign and test in one expression, reducing code repetition.
Trade-offs

Deep nesting — multiple levels of if inside for inside try — makes code hard to read and test. The preferred technique is early return (return from the function as soon as you know the answer) and extraction into helper functions. As a rough heuristic, code nested more than three levels deep is a candidate for refactoring.

Bare except: clauses catch everything including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit, which are usually not what you want. Always catch specific exception types: except ValueError or except (TypeError, ValueError). Use except Exception as e as the outermost fallback only when you genuinely need to catch all non-system exceptions.

While loops with poorly chosen exit conditions can loop forever. Always ensure there is a guaranteed path to the condition becoming False or a break. Use loop counters or timeouts as safety nets in production code.
How Control Flow Works
Conditionals: if, elif, else

Python evaluates conditions left-to-right and stops at the first True branch. The elif keyword prevents nested indentation: instead of if...if...else... you write if...elif...else... as a flat structure. The else clause is optional and runs only when all preceding conditions are False. Conditions can be any expression that evaluates to a truthy or falsy value.

Truthiness in Python: 0, 0.0, None, empty string, empty list, empty dict, empty set, and empty tuple are all falsy. Everything else is truthy. This lets you write if items: instead of if len(items) > 0, which is idiomatic Python.
Loops: for and while

Python's for loop iterates over any iterable — list, tuple, string, dict, set, file, generator, range. The loop variable takes each value in turn; no index arithmetic is needed. When you do need an index, use enumerate(iterable) instead of range(len(iterable)) — it is more readable and avoids index errors.

The while loop repeats its body as long as its condition is True. It is used when the number of iterations is not known in advance — polling a status, retrying a failed operation, consuming a stream until EOF. Always ensure the condition can become False or include a break statement that is guaranteed to trigger.
break, continue, pass

break exits the innermost enclosing loop immediately. continue skips the rest of the current iteration and jumps to the next. pass is a no-op placeholder that satisfies Python's syntactic requirement for a non-empty block.
for-else and while-else

Python loops have an optional else clause that executes only if the loop completed without hitting a break. This is useful for 'search and fail' patterns: search through a list; if the target is found, break; the else clause handles the not-found case.
Architecture Diagram

attrs.alt: Python control flow: if/elif/else decision tree, for loop with break/continue/else, and try/except/else/finally chain

content.text: When to Use Each Construct
Construct
Use when
Common mistake
if/elif/else
Choosing between fixed paths based on a condition
Forgetting elif leads to re-evaluating all conditions
for loop
Iterating over a known sequence or iterable
Using range(len(x)) instead of enumerate(x)
while loop
Repeating until a runtime condition changes
Forgetting the condition can become False (infinite loop)
break
Exiting a loop when a target is found or a limit is hit
Using break in a nested loop only exits the inner loop
continue
Skipping invalid items and continuing the loop
Over-nesting if/else when continue would flatten it
try/except
Handling errors from external systems, parsing, or I/O
Catching Exception too broadly, swallowing real bugs
match/case
Dispatching on the type or structure of a value (3.10+)
Writing fallback wildcard (case _:) last, not first
How to Implement
Conditionals and loop patterns

# --- if / elif / else ---
score = 82

if score >= 90:
    grade = "A"
elif score >= 80:
    grade = "B"
elif score >= 70:
    grade = "C"
else:
    grade = "F"

# Ternary (conditional expression)
status = "pass" if score >= 60 else "fail"

# Truthiness check (idiomatic)
items = []
if not items:
    print("List is empty")   # runs for [] but also for None, 0, "", ...

# Chained comparison (unique to Python)
x = 5
if 1 < x < 10:
    print("x is between 1 and 10")

# Walrus operator (Python 3.8+)
import re
text = "Error: file not found"
if m := re.search(r"Error: (.+)", text):
    print(m.group(1))   # file not found

# --- for loop patterns ---
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]

# Basic iteration
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

# With index (enumerate is idiomatic)
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f"{i}: {fruit}")

# Enumerate with start index
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits, start=1):
    print(f"{i}. {fruit}")   # 1. apple, 2. banana, ...

# Unpack tuples
pairs = [(1, "a"), (2, "b"), (3, "c")]
for num, letter in pairs:
    print(num, letter)

# Iterate over dict
config = {"host": "localhost", "port": 5432}
for key, value in config.items():
    print(f"{key}={value}")

# Range-based loops
for i in range(5):          # 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
    pass
for i in range(2, 10, 2):  # 2, 4, 6, 8
    pass

# --- while loop ---
attempts = 0
max_attempts = 3
success = False

while attempts < max_attempts and not success:
    attempts += 1
    result = "ok" if attempts == 2 else "fail"
    if result == "ok":
        success = True
        break
    print(f"Attempt {attempts} failed, retrying...")

# --- break, continue, pass ---
for num in range(20):
    if num % 3 == 0:
        continue      # skip multiples of 3
    if num > 12:
        break         # stop at 13
    print(num)        # prints 1, 2, 4, 5, 7, 8, 10, 11

# --- for-else (search pattern) ---
target = 7
for num in [2, 5, 9, 11]:
    if num == target:
        print("Found!")
        break
else:
    print("Not found")   # runs because no break was hit
Exception handling
match/case (Python 3.10+)

# --- Structural pattern matching ---
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class ToolCall:
    name: str
    args: dict

def handle_tool_call(call: ToolCall) -> str:
    match call:
        case ToolCall(name="web_search", args={"query": q}):
            return f"Searching for: {q}"
        case ToolCall(name="calculator", args={"expression": expr}):
            return f"Computing: {expr}"
        case ToolCall(name=name) if name.startswith("internal_"):
            return f"Internal tool: {name}"
        case _:
            return f"Unknown tool: {call.name}"

# Match on value types
def classify(value) -> str:
    match value:
        case int() | float():
            return "number"
        case str():
            return "string"
        case list():
            return "list"
        case None:
            return "none"
        case _:
            return "other"

# Match on sequences
def describe_point(point):
    match point:
        case (0, 0):
            return "origin"
        case (x, 0):
            return f"on x-axis at {x}"
        case (0, y):
            return f"on y-axis at {y}"
        case (x, y):
            return f"point at ({x}, {y})"

# Match on dict structures (common for JSON/tool outputs)
def parse_api_response(response: dict) -> str:
    match response:
        case {"status": "ok", "data": data}:
            return f"Success: {data}"
        case {"status": "error", "message": msg}:
            return f"API Error: {msg}"
        case {"status": "rate_limit", "retry_after": seconds}:
            return f"Rate limited. Retry after {seconds}s"
        case _:
            return "Unexpected response format"
Real-world Example: Agent Retry Loop

A production agent must handle transient failures when calling external APIs. This pattern combines while loops, try/except, break, and conditional logic to implement exponential back-off retry with a maximum attempt limit.
Common Mistakes
Using bare except: catches KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit — almost never what you want. Always specify the exception type.
Using range(len(items)) instead of enumerate(items) when you need both index and value.
Modifying a list while iterating over it — can skip elements. Iterate over a copy: for item in list(items).
Forgetting that break only exits the innermost loop. To break out of nested loops, use a flag variable, extract to a function and return, or use a custom exception.
Using while True without a guaranteed break — leads to infinite loops. Add a timeout or iteration count limit.
Writing pass in an except block to silently swallow errors — always at least log the error.
Placing code after return in a function — it is dead code. Python does not warn about this by default.
Using if x == None instead of if x is None. The is operator checks identity; == checks equality and can be overridden.
Best Practices
Prefer early return to flatten nested conditionals: return immediately when input is invalid.
Use enumerate() instead of range(len()) for indexed loops.
Catch specific exceptions, not Exception or bare except.
Always log or re-raise exceptions in except clauses — never silently swallow them.
Use the else clause of a loop for the 'search with no result found' pattern.
Use context managers (with statement) for resource management instead of try/finally.
Limit while loop iterations with a counter or timeout to prevent infinite loops in production.
Use match/case when dispatching on type or structure — it is cleaner than chains of isinstance checks.
Performance and Scalability

Python's for loop over a list is faster than a while loop with manual index arithmetic because the iterator protocol is optimised in CPython. Prefer for item in collection over while i < len(collection). When iterating over large collections, use generators or lazy iterators to avoid loading everything into memory at once.

Exception handling has a near-zero cost when no exception is raised — the try block itself adds negligible overhead. The cost is incurred only when an exception is actually raised and caught, so using try/except for expected failures (e.g., key access in a dict) is fine and often idiomatic in Python (EAFP: Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission).

In hot loops processing millions of items, avoid expensive operations inside the loop: attribute lookups, function calls, and string formatting all add up. Extract constants outside the loop and use local variable references.
Tooling and Ecosystem
itertools module: cycle, repeat, chain, takewhile, dropwhile, groupby — lazy sequence operations that work with for loops.
contextlib.contextmanager: create context managers using generator functions for use in with statements.
tenacity (third-party): decorator-based retry library with back-off strategies — cleaner than hand-rolled retry loops.
Python linters (pylint, flake8, ruff): detect unreachable code, bare except, and common control-flow mistakes.
mypy / pyright: narrow types through if isinstance() checks, enabling full type safety in conditional branches.
Comparison with Alternatives
Task
Idiomatic Python
Avoid
Membership check
if x in collection:
if collection.count(x) > 0:
Indexed loop
for i, v in enumerate(lst):
for i in range(len(lst)): v = lst[i]
Search loop
for x in lst: ... break / else: not found
found = False; for x in lst: if cond: found = True
Error handling
try/except SpecificError:
if condition: (when error is truly exceptional)
Dispatch on type
match val: case int(): ...
if isinstance(val, int): elif isinstance(val, str): ...
Resource cleanup
with open(f) as fh:
try: fh = open(f) ... finally: fh.close()
Security and Reliability
Never use eval() or exec() inside control-flow conditions with user-supplied input — it is a code injection risk.
Sanitise the except clause: catching Exception broadly can hide bugs from unrelated modules that raise exceptions during the try block.
Infinite retry loops without back-off can cause thundering-herd problems: all instances retry simultaneously and overload the downstream service.
Use try/except around external API calls rather than relying on a pre-check (LBYL vs. EAFP) — the check-then-act pattern has a race condition in multi-threaded code.
Set a maximum recursion depth when implementing recursive control flow to avoid stack overflow (sys.setrecursionlimit or switch to iteration).
Mental Model

Think of control flow as road signs on a highway. Sequential code is a straight road. An if/elif/else junction is a multi-way fork — you take exactly one path based on the condition at the sign. A for loop is a roundabout you drive around once per item in the sequence; a while loop is a roundabout you keep circling until the exit condition is met. A break is an emergency exit ramp. A continue is a speed bump that skips you to the next lap without visiting the rest of the roundabout. A try/except block is a parallel service road: if the main road (try body) hits an obstacle (exception), you divert to the service road (except body) and rejoin the highway at the finally block.
Key Takeaways
if/elif/else evaluates conditions in order and takes the first True branch; use the else for the fallback.
for loops iterate over any iterable; use enumerate() when you need both index and value.
while loops repeat until the condition is False; always ensure a termination condition exists.
break exits the innermost loop; continue skips to the next iteration; pass is a no-op placeholder.
The else clause of a loop runs only when the loop completed without a break — useful for search-and-not-found.
try/except/else/finally: try is the risky code, except handles specific errors, else runs on success, finally always runs for cleanup.
match/case (Python 3.10+) provides structural pattern matching — cleaner than chains of isinstance checks.
Catch specific exceptions, never use bare except, and always log or re-raise rather than silently swallowing errors.
